content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
Eliana notices that gasoline prices at nearby stations are nearly identical, but coffee shops charge wildly different prices. Her electric company raised rates, and she has no choice but to pay.

What should be done in this situation?

slides.notes:
**Discussion Prompts:**
- "Why do you think the electric company can charge so much when coffee shops compete for your business?"
- Connect to prior knowledge: Remind students of supply/demand from L-50.
slides.speakerNotes: Read Eliana's scenario aloud or have students read silently. Discussion prompt: "Why do you think the electric company can charge so much when coffee shops compete for your business?" Connect to prior knowledge: Remind students of supply/demand from L-50.

objectives.description: the four main market structures: perfect competition, monopolistic competition, oligopoly, and monopoly
how market structure affects pricing, quality, and consumer surplus
the characteristics that determine market power
market structures in everyday purchases and local industries

terms.definition: The number of sellers and degree of competition in a market
terms.example: Perfect competition (many sellers) vs. monopoly (one seller)

terms.definition: The difference between what you're willing to pay and what you actually pay
terms.example: Willing to pay $10, but only pay $6 = $4 consumer surplus

terms.definition: Obstacles that prevent new firms from entering a market
terms.example: High capital costs, government licenses, economies of scale

terms.definition: A seller who must accept the market price and has no control over pricing
terms.example: A farmer selling wheat at market price

terms.definition: A seller who has power to set prices above competitive levels
terms.example: A utility company with monopoly power

terms.definition: Making a product different from competitors through brand, quality, or features
terms.example: Starbucks charges more due to brand experience

leftPanel.title: Characteristics: Many sellers, identical products, no barriers
paragraphs: Examples: Wheat, corn, stocks, farmer's markets, generic medications

leftPanel.title: Characteristics: Many sellers, differentiated products, low barriers
paragraphs: Examples: Restaurants, coffee shops, clothing stores, hair salons, gyms

infoCard.title: Consumer Strategy
items: Consider if brand premium is worth it

leftPanel.title: Characteristics: Few sellers (3-10), high barriers, significant price control
paragraphs: Examples: Cell carriers (AT&T, Verizon, T-Mobile), airlines, auto manufacturers, streaming services

layoutData.title: One seller, no substitutes, complete price control
paragraphs: Natural Monopoly: One firm can serve entire market more efficiently (electricity, water, gas)
Legal Monopoly: Government grants exclusive right (patents for 20 years, copyrights)
Market Power Monopoly: Firm dominates through business practices (tech giants - debated)
bulletPoints: No choice (take it or leave it)
layoutData.keyPoint: Consumer Strategy: Check for regulated rate structures, use time-of-use pricing, seek long-term substitutes, advocate for regulation

layoutData.title: Classify these common purchases
bulletPoints: Gasoline: Many stations, identical product = Perfect Competition
layoutData.keyPoint: Understanding market structure helps you identify where you have the least power as a consumer

slides.notes:
## Common Misconceptions

| Misconception | Clarification |
|--------------|---------------|
| "Monopolies are always illegal." | Natural monopolies (utilities) and legal monopolies (patents) are legal. Only anticompetitive behavior to gain or maintain monopoly power is illegal. |
| "More sellers always means lower prices." | Generally true, but monopolistic competition shows that differentiation allows price premiums even with many sellers. A branded product can cost 3x a generic. |
| "I can't do anything about monopoly prices." | While you can't change the price, you can reduce consumption, use off-peak pricing, seek substitutes (streaming vs. cable), or advocate for regulation. |
| "Oligopolies compete fiercely on price." | Oligopolies often engage in tacit price coordination. When all 3 carriers charge similar rates, it benefits all firms to keep prices high. |
slides.speakerNotes:
COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS:
• ""Monopolies are always illegal."" → Natural monopolies (utilities) and legal monopolies (patents) are legal. Only anticompetitive behavior to gain or maintain monopoly power is illegal.
• ""More sellers always means lower prices."" → Generally true, but monopolistic competition shows that differentiation allows price premiums even with many sellers. A branded product can cost 3x a generic.
• ""I can't do anything about monopoly prices."" → While you can't change the price, you can reduce consumption, use off-peak pricing, seek substitutes (streaming vs. cable), or advocate for regulation.
• ""Oligopolies compete fiercely on price."" → Oligopolies often engage in tacit price coordination. When all 3 carriers charge similar rates, it benefits all firms to keep prices high.

content.question:
Which market structures affect YOUR budget most?

Where do you have the least power as a consumer? Think about your regular purchases and which ones give you the fewest choices.

scenario.name: Sarah's Coffee Choices
paragraphs: Sarah wants coffee before work. She has several options near her office:
This is monopolistic competition—many sellers, differentiated products, and she has choices!

outcomes.label: Brand Premium
outcomes.detail: Extra cost for Starbucks vs. home brew

outcomes.label: Annual Savings
outcomes.detail: If she skips Starbucks for home brew (260 days)

scenario.name: Oligopoly in Action: Airline Baggage Fees
paragraphs: In 2008, American Airlines added a $25 fee for the first checked bag. Within weeks, United, Delta, and other major carriers matched the fee.
Why? In an oligopoly, firms watch each other closely. No one wants to start a price war, but they'll all match fee increases.
Result: All major airlines now charge $30-35 for first checked bag. Consumers have little choice—all carriers have similar fees.

outcomes.label: Today
outcomes.detail: Per bag on all major carriers

content.question:
Are monopolies always bad for consumers?

Think about natural monopolies like utilities and legal monopolies like patents. When might having just one seller actually benefit society?

leftColumn.title: High Competition (Perfect, Monopolistic)
items: Shop by price - use comparison tools
Ignore marketing hype and brand names
Consider if premium features are worth it
You have power to walk away

questions.question: What are the four main market structures and how do they differ in number of sellers?
Why do firms in an oligopoly often avoid competing on price?
Explain why consumer surplus is highest in perfect competition and lowest in monopoly.
Give three examples of barriers to entry that prevent new firms from entering a market.

slides.notes:
## Answer Key

**1.** B (Monopolistic competition)

**2.** In perfect competition, many sellers compete on price alone, driving prices to the lowest sustainable level—consumers pay minimum and keep maximum surplus. In monopoly, the single seller has complete pricing power and can charge much higher prices, transferring surplus from consumers to the monopolist.

**3.** ($55 − $30) = $25 savings/month. $25 × 12 = **$300 saved per year.**

**4.** OLIGOPOLY (few sellers, similar prices, high barriers). Strategy: Negotiate by threatening to switch, ask for retention discounts, compare all carriers thoroughly, time switch for promotional periods, consider MVNOs (smaller carriers using major networks) for better rates.

**5.** *Reflection should include: specific purchase identified, correct market structure classification, and concrete strategy for improvement (negotiation, shopping around, timing, etc.)*
slides.speakerNotes:
ASSESSMENT ANSWER KEY:
1. B (Monopolistic competition)
2. In perfect competition, many sellers compete on price alone, driving prices to the lowest sustainable level—consumers pay minimum and keep maximum surplus. In monopoly, the single seller has complete pricing power and can charge much higher prices, transferring surplus from consumers to the monopolist.
3. ($55 − $30) = $25 savings/month. $25 × 12 =$300 saved per year.
4. OLIGOPOLY (few sellers, similar prices, high barriers). Strategy: Negotiate by threatening to switch, ask for retention discounts, compare all carriers thoroughly, time switch for promotional periods, consider MVNOs (smaller carriers using major networks) for better rates.
5. Reflection should include: specific purchase identified, correct market structure classification, and concrete strategy for improvement (negotiation, shopping around, timing, etc.).

takeaways.title: Four market structures
takeaways.description: Perfect competition, monopolistic competition, oligopoly, and monopoly differ by number of sellers and barriers to entry

takeaways.title: Market power increases as competition decreases
takeaways.description: From perfect competition (no power) to monopoly (complete power), sellers gain pricing control

takeaways.title: Barriers to entry determine competition
takeaways.description: High capital costs, government licenses, and economies of scale prevent new firms from entering

takeaways.title: Consumer strategies differ by structure
takeaways.description: Shop by price in competitive markets, negotiate in oligopolies, seek substitutes for monopolies

main.title: Analyze Your Local Markets
main.description: Tomorrow you'll identify market structures for YOUR regular purchases and develop consumer strategies for each one

steps: Identify market structures for your regular purchases
Develop consumer strategies for each structure
Analyze local industries by market structure
Evaluate when government intervention makes sense
Use market structure knowledge to negotiate better deals

content.tagline: "Understanding market structure helps you become a smarter consumer and save money"
